coredata.dc:title: Green and microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots and application for visual detection of cobalt(II) ions and pH sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Chunxi
Li, Xiao
Cheng, Chunsheng
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: A facile, economical, and green method for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) was established via microwave irradiation using green natural material kelp as the main carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen dopant. The as-prepared CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), fluorescence spectrophotometer and UV–vis absorption spectra. Furthermore, the CDs showed high sensitivity toward pH and cobalt(II) ion(Co2+). To be specific, the fluorescence intensity of CDs exhibited a linear relationship over the pH range from 3 to 8, and the CDs were applied in the visual detection of Co2+. With the addition of Co2+, the color change (from colorless to brownish yellow under visible light and from bright blue to dark blue under ultraviolet light) of the solution can be clearly seen with the naked eye. Under optimal conditions, the CDs enabled selective detection of Co2+ with a linear range of 1–200 μmol/L and a limit of detection with 0.39 μmol/L. Finally, this method was applied in the analysis of real river water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave-assisted
Carbon dots
Visual detection
Co2+
pH sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and fluorescent properties of CDs
Fluorescence response of CDs toward pH
Selective and sensitive detection of Co2+
Detection of Co2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of pH sensing and detecting Co2+.
(A) TEM image and size distribution of CDs, (B) FTIR spectrum of the CDs, (C) full scan XPS spectrum, (D) XPS spectra of C1s, (E) N1 s, (F) O1s.

(A) UV–vis absorption (red line) and fluorescence excitation (black line) and emission (blue line) spectra of the CDs. Inset: Photographic images of CDs under (1) visible light and (2) ultraviolet light, (B) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 330 to 410 nm.

(A) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of the CDs and different pH values. Inset: the fluorescence spectra of CDs at different pH, (B) fluorescence intensity upon the cyclic switching of CDs under alternating conditions of pH = 3 and pH = 8.
(A) The effect of possible metal ions on the detection of Co2+, (B) photographic images of CDs with different metal ions under visible light (left) and ultraviolet light (right).

The effect of (A) incubation time, (B) pH on the CDs for the detection of Co2+ (the red bars represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs without adjusting pH and the green bars represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs at different pH values), (C) the linear relationship between F/F0 and different concentrations of Co2+ (Inset: the fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of Co2+) and (D) photographic images of CDs with different concentrations of Co2+ under (top) visible light and (down) ultraviolet light.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Co2+ (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of CDs, (B) Fluorescence emission decay curve of CDs and CDs+ Co2+.
Detection of Co2+ in river water samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Green and microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots and application for visual detection of cobalt(II) ions and pH sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Province Food Safety Research Institute, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province, Kunming 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Province Food Safety Research Institute
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile, economical, and green method for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) was established via microwave irradiation using green natural material kelp as the main carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen dopant. The as-prepared CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), fluorescence spectrophotometer and UV–vis absorption spectra. Furthermore, the CDs showed high sensitivity toward pH and cobalt(II) ion(Co2+). To be specific, the fluorescence intensity of CDs exhibited a linear relationship over the pH range from 3 to 8, and the CDs were applied in the visual detection of Co2+. With the addition of Co2+, the color change (from colorless to brownish yellow under visible light and from bright blue to dark blue under ultraviolet light) of the solution can be clearly seen with the naked eye. Under optimal conditions, the CDs enabled selective detection of Co2+ with a linear range of 1–200 μmol/L and a limit of detection with 0.39 μmol/L. Finally, this method was applied in the analysis of real river water samples.

Schematic illustration of pH sensing and detecting Co2+.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt (Co), as an important component of vitamin B12, it is an essential trace element and plays significant roles in the human body. Cobalt could not only stimulate the production of red blood cells, but also regulate the catalytic activity of enzymes or cofactors [1–3]. Besides, cobalt could affect the metabolism of some trace elements [4]. However, excessive intake of cobalt could cause some diseases, such as low blood pressure, diarrhea, asthma, dermatitis, and even death because of myocardial infarction [5,6]. Due to excellent physicochemical properties, cobalt is used in many fields including mining, batteries, catalysts, pigments, paints and so on [7,8]. But the wide application of cobalt also could bring environmental problems, which may be harmful to human health. Therefore, the permissible limits of cobalt in irrigation watering and livestock watering are 0.05 and 1.0 mg L−1, respectively (Environmental Bureau of Investigation, Canadian Water Quality Guidelines) [2]. Up to now, many methods for the detection of Co2+ have be developed, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [9–11], chemiluminescence [12,13], colorimetric [14–16], ICP-OES [17], fluorescence spectroscopy [18,19]. Compared with other detection methods, fluorescence method avoids some disadvantages of other methods such as expensive instrument, crucial pH requirements, complicated sample processing and time-consuming. Owing to these excellent characteristics, the fluorescence method has received more and more attention in the detection of cobalt ion. In addition, as an important parameter, pH plays significant roles in the fields from environmental to industrial to biomedical systems. Therefore, to develop more appropriate pH sensing has become a hot topic. Some studies have reported that several materials including Ag@SiO2 core-shell nanoparticles, quantum dots, encoded red fluorescent protein sensor, semiconducting polymer dots have been developed for pH sensing [20].

Carbon dots (CDs), as one new type of nanomaterials, are usually spherical in shape and with the size below 10 nm [21]. In the past decade or so, CDs have attracted more and more attention due to their excellent properties, including good fluorescence characteristics, water solubility, biocompatibility and low toxicity. Based on the excellent properties, CDs were widely applied in many fields, such as chemical sensing [22–26], LED [27], drug carriers [28–30], cell imaging [31–33] and so on. Furthermore, some CDs exhibit high sensitivity to temperature or pH, and are used for temperature or pH sensing [34,35]. Recently, hydrothermal method has been favored by many researchers for its simple synthesis process [36–38], but the microwave-assisted synthesis method also attracted more and more attention because of its rapid and time-saving advantages [39–41]. In the early days of microwave synthesis, household microwave ovens have been used for chemical synthesis. Nowadays, domestic microwave ovens are usually applied in microwave synthesis, but scientific microwave is also a good choice because of improving reproducibility via temperature and microwave power control, a higher level of safety, the possibility of stirring the solution during microwave irradiation as well as convenient programming and data management. Moreover, the fluorescence and physicochemical properties of CDs largely dependent on the carbon precursors. Chemical substances were usually used as carbon precursors in many studies, while green natural substances have become increasingly popular as carbon sources in recent years due to their environmentally friendly and easily available feature [42–45].

In this work, CDs were synthesized in one pot via microwave irradiation using green natural material kelp as the main carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen dopant. Kelp, as one of seaweed plants, it contains sugar, protein, fiber, mineral salts, calcium, iron and so on. The rich compositions make the CDs which synthesized from kelp have good physical and chemical properties. The as-prepared CDs were characterized and applied in pH sensing and the visual detection of Co2+. With the addition of Co2+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched owing to strong inner filter effect (IFE). In addition, the synthesized CDs were successfully employed for detecting Co2+ in water samples, suggesting their practicability. The process of pH sensing and visual detection of Co2+ by the prepared CDs was shown in Fig. 1 .

CuCl2, FeCl2, MnCl2, NiSO4, FeCl3, HgCl2, Na2HPO4 were purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). HgCl was provided by Guizhou Tongren Chemical Reagent Factory. Citric acid, vitamin C, KCl, NaCl, PbCl2, CdCl2, Cr(NO3)3, K2Cr2O7, CoCl2 were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China). All the stock solutions of metal ions at 1 mM were prepared in ultrapure water. All the reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

The fluorescence measurements were carried out on a G9800A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a TENSOR27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, UK). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was obtained on a D8-advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany).

The CDs were prepared in one pot via microwave irradiation using kelp as the main carbon source. Briefly, 5 g chopped kelp and 0.4mL ethylenediamine were put in the Teflon microwave vessel, and 30 mL of deionized water was added. Then the vessel was tightly sealed and placed in microwave oven. After optical fiber sensor was inserted into the vessel, the temperature of the reaction was risen to 200 °C in 10 min using 800 W microwave irradiations. Reactions proceeded at this temperature for 1.5 h through controlling the duration of microwave irradiation. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min and filtered with 0.22 μm membrane before the dialysis. Finally, the obtained CDs were dialyzed against the pure water for 12 h and stored at 4 °C in refrigerator before used in further experiments.

The quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared CDs was measured with quinine sulfate as the reference according to established methods [46,47]. Quinine sulfate with QY = 0.54 was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) while CDs were dissolved in deionized water. The QY was calculated based on the following equation:
Q =
Q S
×
A S
/
A X
×
I X
/
I S
×
η X
/
η S
2
where Q represents quantum yield, A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, I and η represent the integrated emission intensity and the solvent refraction index, respectively. The subscript “S” refers to quinine sulfate and “X” refers to the CDs. The QY of CDs was calculated to be 23.5%.

In order to evaluate the sensitivity toward Co2+ to the CDs, 200 μL of the as-prepared CDs was added into a centrifuge tube and diluted to 3 mL with deionized water. After the addition of 0.5 mL Na2HPO4-citric acid buffer solution, different concentrations of Co2+ or samples were mixed with the above solution under vortex for about 30 s. Then visual detections were done by observing the color change of the solution. Simultaneously, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and an emission wavelength of 450 nm with 5 nm slits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and fluorescent properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze the morphological structure and composition of the as-synthesized CDs, the CDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR and XPS, respectively. Fig. 2A showed the TEM image and the size distribution of the CDs. It could be observed that the CDs were spheroidal and well dispersed with the average diameter of approximately 3.7 nm. Fig. 2B showed the FTIR spectra. From Fig. 2B we can see that there was a peak at about 3418 cm−1, which was assigned to the stretching vibration of OH and NH. The peak at about 2961 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CH. The peaks at about 1655 cm−1 and 1394 cm−1 indicated the existence of the stretching vibrations of CO and CN. The peak at 1116 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO. The results revealed that the surface of the CDs were rich in hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and amino groups. In addition, the XPS was used to characterize the composition of CDs and the function groups on the surface of CDs. The Fig. 2C was the full scan XPS spectrum of the CDs. The three peaks at 285.2 eV, 399.6 eV and 531.6 eV corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The Fig. 2D showed the C 1s spectrum. The four peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.6 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.2 eV indicated the presence of CC, CN, COH and CO. The Fig. 2E showed the N 1s spectrum. The peaks at 400 eV and 401.4 eV were assigned to CN and NH. The Fig. 2F showed the O 1s spectrum. The two peaks at 531.4 eV and 532.6 eV corresponded to CO and COH. The results also revealed the existence of hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of CDs, which were consistent with the results of FTIR.

Fig. 3A showed the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared CDs. It could be observed that there was a peak at 282 nm in the UV–vis absorption, which was attributed to n-π* transition of CO. Moreover, the fluorescence spectrum showed a maximum emission peak at about 450 nm when excited at 370 nm. Fig. 3B showed the emission spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelength. From Fig. 3B we can see that the CDs exhibited the typical excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission peak obviously shifted to longer wavelength.

The stability evaluation of nanomaterials is very necessary for the detection of target substances. Fig. S1A showed the effect of UV irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The result exhibited that the fluorescence intensity of CDs has almost no significant change under 2 h of ultraviolet radiation. Meanwhile, long-term stability of the as-prepared CDs at 4 °C was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1B, the CDs could be stable for at least 2 months, which was very beneficial for study. Furthermore, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was also investigated by the addition of different concentrations of NaCl. As shown in Fig. S1C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains almost unchanged as the concentration of NaCl increases. The result revealed that the CDs can be stable in a medium of comparatively high ionic strength.

The stability evaluation of nanomaterials is very necessary for the detection of target substances. Fig. S1A showed the effect of UV irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The result exhibited that the fluorescence intensity of CDs has almost no significant change under 2 h of ultraviolet radiation. Meanwhile, long-term stability of the as-prepared CDs at 4 °C was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1B, the CDs could be stable for at least 2 months, which was very beneficial for study. Furthermore, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was also investigated by the addition of different concentrations of NaCl. As shown in Fig. S1C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains almost unchanged as the concentration of NaCl increases. The result revealed that the CDs can be stable in a medium of comparatively high ionic strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs toward pH
ce:section.ce:para: In the investigation of the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, we found that the CDs were highly sensitive to the pH values and possess a pH-sensitive fluorescence feature. Different pH values of the CDs solution were adjusted with Na2HPO4-citric acid buffer solution. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and an emission wavelength of 450 nm with 5 nm slits. As shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensities of CDs decrease with the increase of pH values. Surprisingly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs and pH values were in a good linear relationship in the range of pH 3–8. The linear equation was F = −30.143pH +741.28 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9897. In addition, Fig. 4B showed that the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared CDs was reversible during continuous at least five cycles of acidification and alkalization over the pH range 3–8. The most possible mechanism for pH-sensitive fluorescence feature of the CDs was the reversible protonation/deprotonation of the CDs in acidic and alkaline condition. At lower pH, the strong vibration coupling of –OH functional groups caused by H-bond binding can lead to energy level broadening and increase the conformational rigidity of the CDs. Therefore, the fluorescent intensity of the CDs was strong at acid. But the eliminated H-bonding effect upon deprotonation at alkaline conditions may lead to the reduced vibrational coupling and more discrete energy levels, which bring about the decrease of fluorescence intensity of the CDs [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Co2+
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of the CDs was crucial for determination of Co2+ in real water samples. To confirm the selectivity of the CDs toward Co2+, the interferences from other metal ions were estimated. As shown in Fig. 5A, the possible coexisting metal ions including K+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Na+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cr3+almost have no effect on the fluorescence of the CDs except for the Cr6+. However, we found that Cr3+ has little effect on the fluorescence of CDs, so it is possible to remove the interference of Cr6+ by adding vitamin C as reducing agent to reduce Cr6+ to Cr3+. Importantly, results showed that vitamin C has no influence on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5B, it is interesting to note that the color of CDs with Co2+ changed to brownish yellow while this phenomenon did not appear when the CDs were added with other metal ions. This method can achieve rapid identification of Co2+. In the study, the fluorescence of the as-prepared CDs was quenched with the addition of Co2+. In order to achieve the maximum response of Co2+ to CDs, some tests were implemented to optimize the sensing parameters, including the incubation time and pH of detection system. The effect of incubation time was shown in Fig. 6A. It could be observed that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains almost unchanged after 3 min of Co2+ addition and the state can last for at least half an hour. Considering fast and efficient detection, the fluorescence quenching time of 3 min was chosen for incubation. Fig. 6B exhibited the effect of pH on the detection system. The effect of pH on the CDs was discussed before, and we could also observe that pH had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs when pH was about 7. It cannot affect the detection of Co2+. Therefore, pH 7 was chosen for the pH value of detection system. As shown in Fig. 6C, under the optimized conditions, a good linear relationship between F/F0 and Co2+ concentration was obtained in the range of 1–200 μmol/L with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9912. The linear equation was F/F0 = −0.0028C + 0.9674. Where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence and absence of Co2+, respectively. C represents the concentration of Co2+. The detection limit was 0.39 μmol/L(3σ/k). Simultaneously, as shown in Fig. 6D, the color change (from colorless to brownish yellow under visible light and from bright blue to dark blue under ultraviolet light) of the solution with the addition of Co2+ can be clearly seen with the naked eye (the concentrations of Co2+ were 10, 25, 50, 100, 200 μmol/L, respectively). Consequently, it could be achieved to detect Co2+ selectively and sensitively by combining colorimetric and fluorescent spectroscopy.

It can be found that the fluorescence of CDs could be selectively quenched by Co2+, revealing the possibility of applying the as-prepared CDs as a sensitive fluorescent chemosensor for the detection of Co2+. In order to explore the possible quenching mechanism, some optical properties of CDs and Co2+ were investigated. Fig. 7A showed the UV absorption spectrum of Co2+ and the fluorescence spectrum of the as-prepared CDs. A peak at 510 nm was exhibited in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Co2+, while the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of CDs were at 370 nm and 450 nm, respectively. It could be observed that there was a good spectral overlap between the UV absorption spectrum of Co2+ and the emission spectrum of the CDs, implying the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs by Co2+ is inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [49]. In order to verify which one is the most possible fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of the CDs in the presence and absence of Co2+ were recorded. As shown in Fig. 7B, the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of the CDs both in the presence and absence of Co2+ were unchanged, suggesting FRET was excluded. Therefore, the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs by Co2+ was IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicality, the proposed method was applied to determine the Co2+ in river water samples. The river water samples were obtained from local river water. All the samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm and filtered through 0.22 μm membrane before analysis. The standard addition recovery experiment was performed in this research. Results were shown in Table 1 . The recoveries were in the range 95.0–106.8%, and the RSD were below 5.3%. These results indicated that the proposed method exhibited high accuracy, precision and great potential for reliable detection of Co2+ in river water samples.

In summary, the CDs were synthesized in one pot via microwave irradiation using green natural material kelp as the main carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen dopant. The as-prepared CDs exhibited good water solubility and excellent optical properties. The CDs not only exhibited high sensitivity to pH but also showed strong selectivity and sensitivity for Co2+ detection. On the one hand, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased as pH values increased within pH 3–8, and was in a good linear relationship with different pH values. On the other hand, with the addition of Co2+, the color of CDs changed from colorless to brownish yellow while it did not change in the presence of other mental ions. Furthermore, the fluorescence of CDs can be selectively quenched by Co2+ and the fluorescence quenching was in a good linear relationship with the concentration of Co2+ in the range of 1–200 μmol/L. The detection limit was 0.39 μmol/L. The successful application of Co2+ detection in real water samples indicated that this method had great application prospects in the detection of Co2+.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
The effect of (A) UV irradiation time, (B) Placement time and (C) NaCl ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2019.03.029.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was strongly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (2017M20162118092).
